content.full_text:
V 2012(b-)

976-04-064

April 23, 1975

MR. W. Dean Cannon, Jr., Treasurer
Committee for the Support of ACA 31
9300 South Sepulveda Blvd., Suite 908
Los Angeles, CA 90315

RE: Request for Opinion
    No. 75-001

Dear Mr. Cannon:

This letter is to confirm a telephone conversation I
had with John Lannan on Wednesday, April 21, in which I gave
him informal advice on the reporting obligation of the ACA
31 Committee and various individual Savings and Loan associations. Pending the issuance of a formal opinion, I hope
this advice will be helpful. If the formal opinion which
you have requested reaches results different from the advice
contained in this letter, the general policy of the Commission
is to apply such opinion prospectively. Furthermore, I
apologize for the long delay in answering the question in
your opinion request but the response has been delayed purposely until the Commission can complete action on several
relevant regulations.

With respect to the mailing of inserts, the effort and
expense of the member associations will constitute both inkind contributions and expenditures which, if in excess of
$500, will cause the member association to become a committee
pursuant to Government Code Section 82013(b). An associations
expenses are an in-kind contribution because they represent
goods and services which will be rendered to the committee
under its control and direction. These expenses also will
represent expenditures by the member associations in that
they will be costs incurred in connection with the making of
a communication which expressly advocates a position with
respect to a clearly identified measure.

In determining whether the $500 threshold has been met,
each member association should include the cost of supplies
and postage reasonably attributable to the "political" portion of the mailing. In this particular case, the member
associations need not [illegible] costs related to employee
time which may be spent assisting in the mailing. Once this

(5)

RE: N. Dean Cannon, jr.
April 23, 1976
Page Two

figure is computed, it should be communicated to your committee
for disclosure on your campaign statement as in-kind contributions
received.

In the present case you also need not view those mailings
as "mass mailings" subject to the provisions of Government
Code Section 84305. Accordingly, the member associations
need not observe any particular costing requirements and a
copy of the mailing insert need not be forwarded to the
Commission.

Lastly, none of the expenses incurred in connection
with setting up or displaying countersigns need be reported
by your committee or any member association. Since Mr. Lannan
indicated you would not be displaying window signs this
letter does not address any issues which might be raised by
the display of such signs. Again, I apologize for the delay
in responding to your formal opinion request. If I can be
of any further assistance, please contact me at (916) 322-
3747.

Sincerely,


Ted Prim
Staff Counsel

TP:clh /

embedding.qa_text: April 23, 1976 Mr. W. Dean Cannon, Jr., Treasurer Committee for the Support of ACA 31 9800 South Sepulveda Blvd., Suite 500 Los Angeles, CA 90015 Re: Request for Opinion No. 76-001 Dear Mr. Cannon: This letter is to confirm a telephone conversation I had with John Lannan on Wednesday, April 21, in which I gave him informal advice on the reporting obligation of the ACA 31 Committee and various individual Savings and Loan Associations. Pending the issuance of a formal opinion, I hope this advice will be helpful. If the formal opinion which you have requested reaches results different from the advice contained in this letter, the general policy of the Commission is to apply such opinion prospectively. Furthermore, I apologize for the long delay in answering the question in your opinion request but the response has been delayed purposely until the Commission can complete action on several relevant regulations. With respect to the mailing of inserts, the effort and expense of the member associations will constitute both in-kind contributions and expenditures which, if in excess of $500, will cause the member association to become a committee pursuant to Government Code Section 82013(b). An association's expenses are an in-kind contribution because they represent goods and services which will be rendered to the committee under its control and direction. These expenses also will represent expenditures by the member associations in that they will be costs incurred in connection with the making of a communication which expressly advocates a position with respect to a clearly identified measure. In determining whether the $500 threshold has been met, each member association should include the cost of supplies and postage reasonably attributable to the "political" portion of the mailing. In this particular case, the member associations need not report any costs related to employee time which may be spent assisting in the mailing. Once this figure is computed, it should be communicated to your committee for disclosure on your campaign statement as in-kind contributions received. In the present case you also need not view these mailings as "mass mailings" subject to the provisions of Government Code Section 84305. Accordingly, the member associations need not observe any particular posting requirements and a copy of the mailing insert need not be forwarded to the Commission. Lastly, none of the expenses incurred in connection with setting up or displaying countersigns need be reported by your committee or any member association. Since Mr. Lannan indicated you would not be displaying window signs this letter does not address any issues which might be raised by the display of such signs. Again, I apologize for the delay in responding to your formal opinion request. If I can be of any further assistance, please contact me at (916) 322-5747. Sincerely, Ted Prim Staff Counsel TP:plh
